data.title: LCOW: The Backdoor To Quality Tech And AI
data.description:
"Remember: cash is a fact, profit is an opinion." Alfred Rappaport, author of "Creating Shareholder Value."

According to Morningstar, there are *48* AI-themed ETFs available for purchase, with more yet to come.

I don't believe there are 48 individual AI companies worth buying! Yet the ETFs just keep on a-comin'. Very few of them differ in any significant way. You only have to look at the top holdings of all of these, and you will often see that the biggest difference is only the sponsor's name.

Thank you, Pacer, for creating something I believe is being overlooked in this industry. It isn't only about how many chips a company can produce. What if your revenues soar because you are selling, selling, selling masses of your product? That's great, but are you bringing those revenues to the bottom line as a profit, or are you running in place?

An Easy Analogy

Think of two people, one an optimistic entrepreneur, the other a hard worker.

Lady X has income ("revenue", to a corporation) of $225,000 a year from her own business. But she lives in a high-tax state that takes 10% or more off the top. Then there are Federal taxes to be paid. And she has to commute across a big city, regularly stuck in traffic. Twice a month, she flies around the country to keep her clients happy. She entertains frequently at the best restaurants to maintain her image, and so on and so on.

At the end of the year, maybe she has $50,000 in "profits". She did have cash flow. But it flowed in both directions, leaving her wondering why she couldn't afford to live on her huge income. She likely has no "free cash flow" at the end of the year.

Gentleman Y has recently left the Navy as a Machinist's Mate. He scores a fine job with a good salary of $85,000 working in a defense company. He lives in one of the nine states with zero state income tax (as do 22% of Americans). He commutes to his job in less than half an hour, never has to fly anywhere except for fun, does no expensive entertaining, but enjoys time with his family, friends, and neighbors.

He might only have $5,000 at the end of the year, but he invests it every single year. He has "free cash flow."

It is the same with the companies you and I invest in

"Operating Cash Flow" (OCF) usually refers to the revenue generated from the sale of products or services.

"Free Cash Flow" (FCF) is what remains after the company pays for operating expenses such as rent or maintenance on owned buildings, equipment purchased or maintained, and employees hired.

In the AI business, this would include paying for top talent, research and development, physical assets to produce the product, and those huge data centers.

Strong FCF shows the business is self-funding. It is the opposite of eating its young; it is birthing new ideas and products, funded by a strong income stream.

For AI competitors, the gap between OCF and FCF can be massive.

Moving beyond considering only AI and other technologies, Pacer is all about "cash cows." They have been ever since the inception of their first ETFs.

Why is 60% of This "Quality" Fund in Tech and AI?

Fair question. Aren't these companies more risky? Short answer: No. Not necessarily.

First of all, this relatively new fund doesn't seek out AI and Tech stocks. It is simply a fact that today companies in this business do have large free cash flows and have had for at least a decade. The Pacer S&P 500 Quality FCF Aristocrats ETF might look entirely different in 6 months or a year. This is not a static fund.

LCOW screens the S&P 500 to identify the top 100 companies with at least 10 consecutive years of positive free cash flow, and the highest combination of FCF margin and FCF return on invested capital.

High hurdles. And hence the "Aristocrat" in the title. The companies selected tend to have high financial stability and operating strength and are efficient allocators of their capital. The results are likely to include long-term capital appreciation with financially sound companies.

Here is LCOW's selection process works:

Too many investors are awestruck when they see increasing revenue numbers. But if you review FCF and see that it is regularly lower than the Net Income numbers, you should be cautious: the company might well be finessing its profitability via accounting maneuvers (while losing actual cash).

Is This Why Warren Buffett Held Apple for So Long?

Apple's (AAPL) business model has always been to ensure customers pay in advance, but for Apple to pay its suppliers later, as the purchases start rolling in. This effectively uses "other people's money" to pay salaries, conduct research, and produce new products. Apple's stock might be unchanged so far this year, but I would not count them out!

Alphabet (GOOG) (GOOGL) is another Free Cash Flow generator. GOOG can afford its hefty projected $45 billion or more in CapEx (capital expenditures) this year because its search engine generates a "free cash flow" surplus that allows for billions in CapEx, share buybacks, and even its first dividends.

Nvidia (NVDA) doesn't build chips. It creates the design, then hands actual production off to Taiwan Semi (TSM) and others. Being "fabless" is fabulous - this is what lets NVDA maintain hardware-like dominance with software-like cash margins.

I use these three as examples. They are among LCOW's top holdings. The other top holdings have similar profiles.

Here are the top 20 holdings as of today:

Sector Allocation

As you can see below, roughly 65% of LCOW's current holdings are in either the Communication Services sector, like GOOG, or in Technology, like NVDA. But if somehow it should "all fall down" in the tech world, LCOW is rebalanced semi-annually.

Why not more frequently? After all, if AI looks to be a bust, won't we see it in less than 6 months?

I think there is a better question to ask. If, in fact, there is a decline in Free Cash Flow, the team at Pacer would be looking to change horses. But as long as the companies in the current mix continue to dominate in all the other areas they are involved in, will the current leaders remain Aristocrats?

I believe the answer is yes. And because this is a broader ETF than solely tech and AI, I think Mastercard (MA), Visa (V), AbbVie (ABBV), Johnson & Johnson (JNJ), et al., will provide a buffer against such an unlikely - but possible - fall from grace. Remember, this is not an AI fund locked into its positions. It is holdings-agnostic. May the best companies rise to the top.

Risk Factors

First up, of course, is the (low?) possibility of a sudden plunge in the value of the Pacer S&P 500 Quality FCF Aristocrats ETF's largest holdings, resulting from an equally sizable decline in shares of technology and AI companies.

Next, LCOW, a small fund. Place limit orders!

This is also a relatively new ETF. Not even a year old, if your due diligence suggests you should own LCOW, remember that there are very few shares in each of its investments, though of course that will change as more investors buy the ETF.

Finally, a serious market decline will destroy the share prices for almost all US-listed firms.

My Defense Against That Last Risk Factor

I have been in this business long enough to have seen many panic declines. My best refuge has always been owning some materials as we reach higher and higher, and I always own shares of high-quality non-U.S. companies that are easy to buy and sell on U.S. exchanges.

In this vein, I invite you to take a look at the London Stock Exchange Group (LSEGY), which I analyzed just yesterday. I think you will be shocked to learn that this powerhouse is going head-to-head with Bloomberg and is a massive competitor in many other "non-exchange" financial services.

In times of potential crisis, I also own copper, silver, gold, and other essential materials. But that is the subject of other forthcoming analyses. Thank you for reading, and I look forward to your comments!

"If you don't have the free cash flow, you don't have anything." - Leon Cooperman, Founder of Omega Advisors

data.title: CAGE:CA ETF: Wait For Outperformance Before Buying
data.description:
CAGE:CA at a Glance

The Avantis CIBC All-Equity Asset Allocation ETF (CAGE:CA) is a new actively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) with a NAV of ~$100MM CAD that invests in stocks designed to provide exposure to a portfolio of global stocks. CIBC recently notched a deal with Avantis to bring some of their actively managed expertise and branding into the fund space. Avantis, based in the USA, was spun out of American Century and has >$100 billion in AUM. At the time of the alliance announcement, it piqued my curiosity, as CIBC has historically not ventured into asset management with a heavy marketing push, unlike some other Big 5. While CIBC has many publicly available ETFs, partnering with Avantis, known for value factor funds, highlights a strategic push to acquire customers in its wealth management business. This new offering is part of that outreach—though I'm not convinced it's differentiated enough to break through in the Canadian market.

CAGE:CA's benchmark target is the MSCI ACWI IMI Index, which captures large-, mid-, and small-cap representation across developed and emerging market countries. Right off the bat, this is one of the more normal indexes to track, and it makes me a bit skeptical of how differentiated the fund is willing to go. Peers in this space include VEQT:CA, XEQT:CA, and ZEQT:CA, all established funds from Vanguard, BlackRock, and BMO, respectively. The bottom line here is that Canada is not a big market—if fund managers want to attract new capital into their funds, highlighting an alliance with a solid active fund manager from the U.S. is not enough. For now, I say keep an eye on this fund, but it's not worth buying until further details emerge.

Fund Profile

CAGE:CA was launched on March 18, 2026, by CIBC and Avantis with a plan to create a diversified, easily tradable fund that selects companies based on value and profitability characteristics, using a broad set of company financial fundamentals such as book value, earnings, and cash flow. At a glance, it is an interesting proposition. Avantis brings a factor-focused philosophy to its actively managed funds and generally tilts toward small caps and uses book value and profitability screeners within their go-to methodologies. In fact, within the main brochure, the fund manager appears to be pitching how the Avantis filter will try to gain an edge against the broader index.

Fund of Funds Structure

The fund is set up in a fund-of-funds structure, similar to other Avantis funds. That means it is simply repackaging this branded fund and pulling from other existing offerings in specific weights. I generally don't like this move, as more funds involved lead to slight derivatives in both performance and expectations versus the initial mandate. But what disappoints me is the expected weights of this fund. Where the MSCI ACWI IMI Index is a true global index, with Canada around 3-5% weight, this fund expects to hold 30% Canadian equities… just like all their peers. VEQT:CA, XEQT:CA, and ZEQT:CA all hold ~25%-30% in Canadian equity… and based on my research, I could not find many, if any, peers that truly track the MSCI ACWI Index in CAD. What seemed like part of the marketing pitch turns out to be a common ETF, with potentially some deviation based on the other fund performances, but mostly in line with what is already out there.

Early Returns

The fund has only been trading for about a month, so I can't draw many insights, but early returns haven't been great. It has somehow slightly underperformed all of its peers over the past month. Not a huge deal, and liquidity/spreads likely play a role in this, but it's not a great start.

When I look at some underlying holdings in the fund, like Avantis CIBC U.S. All-Cap Equity ETF (CAUS:CA) and Avantis CIBC Canadian Equity ETF (CACE:CA), they essentially look the same as peers, but with the value and profitability filter. When driving down into what an investor is truly paying for, they are not paying for different stocks in the fund. In fact, they will likely be similar to current market offerings. But reading between the lines, weightings may be slightly different among the stocks within the fund given different book value characteristics and profitability scores—and ok, it might work over the long haul. But at higher fees, lower liquidity, and mixed results, I'm still a bit wary.

This fund is also charging a fee that's a bit higher for basically the same holdings (0.28% vs. 0.17-0.24% for the other peers). And in terms of liquidity, CAGE:CA trades about 140,000 shares per day worth ~$3MM; not ideal for whales due to lack of liquidity, but not bad in its first month. This is an important note, as many funds in Canada, given the lack of institutional and retail ownership, are thinly traded, leading to higher bid/ask spreads and mispricing of the ETF units against the underlying holdings.

Key Risks

From a risk perspective, I can't add quantitative data given its recent start, but it's something I will keep an eye on. The main risk with the fund is the global economy. The Iran war and tariff uncertainty lead the pack, though the tariff risk is somewhat mitigated with the Supreme Court knocking down tariffs. While companies have eaten a good chunk of the tariffs over the past few months, no matter how you slice it, corporate earnings or higher inflation will emerge, and extended tariffs should lead to a material dip in the market, prompting potential dividend cuts along with stock price drops. Ultimately, the fund managers managing to their mandate is another risk, though a quick glance online didn't reveal any red flags in this department.

Bottom Line

Ultimately, the bottom line is that this fund doesn't wow me at a glance. The fund has a higher expense ratio, has underperformed peers (albeit over a month), and doesn't have enough differentiation from well-known peers. The Canadian market isn't big enough to have multiple players doing the same thing. Maybe the active management and fund-of-funds structure allow these managers to gain an edge, but until that's proven, I'd say keep this on the watch list and rate it a Hold.

data.title: Hexcel Corporation: Operating Leverage Finally Arrives, Strong Buy
data.description:
Hexcel Corporation (HXL), the provider of composite materials for the aerospace and defense industry, reported Q1 2026 financial results that surpassed the estimates of analysts on the top and bottom line. The stock price has increased nearly 12% since my last report, sharply outperforming the S&P 500’s 3.5% return. While we see end market growth, there is also cost inflation risk, as I discuss in this report in support of my continued buy rating.

Hexcel Operating Leverage Kicks In

In the fourth quarter, Hexcel sales rose 9.9% and 8.8% on constant currency to $501.5 million. Operating income grew 30.3% to $57.6 million, with margins improving from 9.9% to 13.5%. This clearly signaled operating leverage, but we note that operating expenses as a share of revenue were higher year-on-year, with SG&A accounting for 9.9% of revenues compared to 9.5% a year ago and R&D accounting for 3.5% of revenues compared to 3% a year ago. In other words, we are seeing improving sales leverage on the gross margin level, but operating cost absorption is not yet happening in a favorable way.

Commercial aerospace sales during the quarter grew 18.8% to $332.7 million, driven by the two wide body programs, namely the Airbus A350 and Boeing 787, and two single aisle programs, which are the Boeing 737 MAX and Airbus A320neo. The Airbus A350 and Boeing 787 programs have high-value composite shipset values, while the Boeing 737 MAX and Airbus A320neo are value drivers through volume. For the twelve-month trailing period, we see that sales rose 4.5% and are at a multi-year high. Having the big picture in mind is good, but we also note that by presenting twelve-month trailing figures, the company may at times not highlight quarterly sales improvements enough. This was such a quarter, as LTM figures show 4.5% growth while quarterly figures show nearly 19% growth.

Hexcel has embedded its shrinking Industrial business into the Defense and Space segment. I believe that is not a wise thing to do, as these are distinctly different markets. Defense, Space and Other sales decreased 4.3% to $168.8 million, with the sales decline driven by the divestiture of an Industrial business in Austria in September 2025 and lower space composite sales for launchers and rocket motors, while defense sales were lifted by fighter jet and military helicopter programs.

2026 Is Shaping Up To Be A Good Year But With Some Risks

For the full year, Hexcel is expecting $2-$2.1 billion in sales; this would mark high single-digit to low-double-digit sales growth. EPS is expected to be $2.10-$2.30 with free cash flow bigger than $195 million, marking around 25% growth in free cash flow generation.

Generally, 2026 is shaping up to be a good year for Hexcel as gross margins are improving with the potential for better operating cost absorption. At the same time, we note that the war in Iran is bringing some risks. Higher oil prices could raise energy and logistics costs as well as feedstock costs. On propylene, which Hexcel uses for composite manufacturing, the company hedges for eight quarters, meaning that the company is partially shielded in the near term from higher input costs. The company also uses a local-for-local setup where facilities in Europe produce for the European market and facilities in the US produce for the US market. Similarly, their inputs are also concentrated in the areas of production, limiting immediate supply chain risks.

Hexcel Corporation Remains Attractive on Aerospace Growth

To determine multi-year price targets, The Aerospace Forum has developed a stock screener that uses a combination of analyst consensus on EBITDA, cash flows, and the most recent balance sheet data. Each quarter, we revisit those assumptions and the stock price targets accordingly. In a separate blog, I have detailed our analysis methodology.

I am upgrading Hexcel Corporation stock to a strong buy with a $105.52 price target, indicating 16% upside. That may not seem like upside that would justify a strong buy rating. However, we note that improving commercial airplane production rates in combination with growing defense demand brings the sales CAGR to around 10%, with EBITDA growing 17% annually through 2028 and free cash flow growing 26% annually. So, the operating leverage is expected to sharply improve while Hexcel trades at a discount to peers.

EBITDA margins dipped in 2025 due to destocking activities among commercial airplane manufacturers as production rate increases were softer than expected. However, the current expectation is that Hexcel will be able to substantially increase its operating leverage, and that would drive margins to north of 20% with strong free cash flow generation opportunities and capital expenditures moderating against operating cash flow. My assumption is that the company will maintain share repurchase activity while simultaneously also seeing its net debt leverage improve,

Conclusion: Hexcel Is Finally Showing Operating Leverage

For Hexcel, there are some cost inflation risks due to the war in Iran, but I do not expect demand to fall through. So, we are watching the cost development, but note that Hexcel has partial shielding against rising costs. Furthermore, we see that operating leverage is substantially improving as airplane production rates increase, and that should be a key driver to free cash flow. I am upgrading Hexcel from "buy" to "strong buy" on its strong growth prospects while being undervalued against peers.

data.title: Alphabet Q1 Preview: Why The $4 Trillion Market Cap Still Offers Some Value
data.description:
My last analysis of Alphabet Inc. (GOOG) (GOOGL) (check it here) was at Q4 earnings, and since then the stock dropped a bit but has already recovered and is up about 8%. And now with Q1 approaching (next week, on the 29th), it seems like the best time to revisit Alphabet’s thesis, assessing risks and mainly thinking about whether this valuation of more than $4 trillion makes sense.

To jump to my conclusion, I do think it makes sense, mainly in the long run. Alphabet has truly proven that it deserves this “new” valuation with a certain premium instead of being a stock that trades at a massive discount compared to other big techs, as was the case a few years ago. But at the same time, I am a bit more cautious about what the market may think in the Q1 earnings, since with this volatile environment and a higher valuation level, drawdowns also become somewhat more likely.

Alphabet’s Valuation Still Makes Sense

In my article “Betting Against Alphabet? You’ll Need A Better Bear Case.” I attached the following table (check the full article here):

Even with recent advancements, I do not think it is a completely impossible scenario, but certainly in the short term this scenario has already become obsolete and unlikely. Search has shown very healthy growth, subscriptions as well, and Cloud delivered an impressive growth. Q4 was excellent, and with all the investments Alphabet is making in infrastructure, this growth should continue for some time.

So I improved my outlook for the coming years. As you can see in the table below, I brought Search assumption back to a more moderate scenario, growing 10% in 2026 and declining to mid-single digits. I kept similar expectations for Subscriptions, Network, and YouTube, and for Cloud I increased growth considerably given the scale of investments I mentioned.

But as you can see, the multiples (price-to-operating income) are higher than before. This is because now the market is assigning a relevant premium to Alphabet; therefore, the market cap increased, and multiples also increased.

Still, even in this scenario of a $4 trillion market cap, I do not think Alphabet is extremely overvalued or anything like that. The ~17x operating income in 2030 assumes a very weak Search momentum. If AI continues proving to be a tailwind without cannibalizing revenue for 5 years, we are talking about something closer to 16x-15x, and margins could also continue improving and exceed the estimated 35%. It is worth remembering that operating income here also acts as a proxy for shareholder value. In 2030, Alphabet may end up generating more cash flow than it does now since depreciation and other expenses do not impact cash flow, just as it may generate less cash for shareholders if CapEx remains high.

The market expects even better numbers than these, which reinforces how these assumptions are somewhat conservative. Revenue for 2030 is close to $770 billion, and the difference is mainly that the market assumes stronger momentum for 2028-2030, keeping numbers above 10% even in that period. With that, the market sees a P/E of 15.5x for 2030.

As I mentioned, my table is more of a proxy; it is not something I want to get exactly right but rather to assess the margin of safety. And even in this scenario that I consider between conservative and base, Alphabet still makes sense in valuation terms, since 17x operating income for a company leading in AI, growing EPS in low double digits or high single digits, with tons of optionality, is cheap.

Think that if in 2029 GOOGL stock is trading at 25x forward operating income, that is a 43% appreciation in 3 years. It is not extraordinary, but it is already interesting.

Q1 Earnings: What Could Go Wrong

But the reason I am a bit more cautious is that I think the path to 2030 will have turbulence.

One reason is a mix of higher valuation and the market’s short-term focus. Even though I am bullish on Alphabet’s case and also on Q1, I think the market may end up disliking some aspects.

The first and most obvious is CapEx. In the last Q4, Alphabet already mentioned the large amount of CapEx it intends to spend this year, and Q1 will be a quarter where we will get a taste of that. Even though the 2025 CapEx was not low, free cash flow was still quite positive. Cash from operations was $164 billion, and CapEx was $91 billion. The same happened in Q4. Now think of a scenario where, in Q1, CapEx alone consumes ~80%-100% of cash flow from operations; the market may react negatively and trigger a sell-off.

Currently, GOOGL stock is trading at a forward P/E close to 30x. Just returning to the average of 23x would imply a drawdown of around 20%. If the market really dislikes the results, we could see part of this “full drawdown potential.”

This would be negative for the stock, but personally it would have very little impact on my long-term view of Alphabet.

But this is not the only thing that could go wrong in Q1. Google Search remains very important, so if numbers come in much worse than what we have been seeing recently, or if there is any indication that AI is no longer a tailwind for the search business—that is, if they say Gemini is growing but cannibalizing search, that would be concerning in the medium term. In fact, it is even more complex than that. Even if AI revenue starts to appear, for example, subscriptions with Gemini, but margins cannot keep up due to high costs, we may have greater uncertainty in Alphabet’s case and again a potential drawdown.

Even news about regulation, such as updates on legal cases or any one-off impact on the financials could happen.

Q1 Earnings: My Take Is That It Will Be Solid

But even with this concern about perception, I think Q1 earnings will be solid. Particularly, I think what would be most interesting as a positive surprise is Search. If it maintains solid growth or even accelerates slightly, proving that “AI mode” and new Google features are increasing engagement and consequently revenue, that would be very bullish for the stock.

Another point is Google Cloud. Even if CapEx is a delicate issue, if this business division maintains strong top-line growth, with healthy margins and a strong backlog, it will be an excellent signal that the business is sustainable and that demand is real and consistent.

Just these two points could already drive a double beat for Q1.

The market expects revenue of $106.9 billion, 18% growth. In Q4 Alphabet not only delivered 18% YoY growth but also delivered $113 billion in revenue. So even considering seasonality and similar factors, delivering something close to $110 billion seems completely possible.

For EPS, the market expects some decline due to the comparison base. But even here Alphabet may surprise if there is any EPS trigger, such as an updated valuation of SpaceX as seen in previous quarters, lower losses from optionalities such as Waymo, or even better Cloud margins.

In short, I am optimistic about Q1. I believe Alphabet will be able to show that the infrastructure they are building is not in vain, that the Cloud business is indeed growing sustainably; also prove that the core is not only solid but growing; and finally, they will provide good operational updates in the earnings call, whether on Search, YouTube, Subscriptions, or even “Other Bets,” that further strengthen the long-term case.

Bottom Line: Short-Term Volatility, Long-Term Strength

Well, as I believe is clear, in the short term anything can happen. Even if Alphabet delivers a double beat next week, nothing prevents the market from pushing the stock down 5% just because it dislikes pressured free cash flow or something similar. And this becomes even more true when Alphabet’s multiples are already above historical averages, so yes, I have a cautious tone.

But while I expect volatility in the short term, I expect strength in the long term. I would not see a problem if the stock does not rise or even declines somewhat, as long as results remain solid and management can increase my conviction that the long term is promising. Therefore, I maintain my buy rating on Alphabet stock.

data.title: Whale's Insight: If BTC's Top Was Pulled Forward, Why Not The Bottom?
data.description:
The four-year cycle is mutating. Volatility is compressing, returns are shrinking, and the old playbook no longer fits. But behind the surface, institutions are building positions through ETFs, equity, and preferred stock. Meanwhile, on-chain LTH supply is rising again and shorts remain under pressure.

The Mutating Cycle: What Institutions Are Telling Us

The Four-Year Cycle Is Mutating

Measured from the April 2024 halving, Bitcoin's (BTC-USD) current cycle has dramatically underperformed every prior epoch.

The 2012 cycle delivered roughly 9,300% in gains, the 2016 cycle around 2,950%, and the 2020 cycle about 760%. By contrast, the rally from the fourth halving peaked at roughly 97% above the halving-day price, an order-of-magnitude step down from any predecessor.

This is not just a matter of smaller percentage moves on a larger base.

The 30-day realized volatility tells a parallel story:

  • 2020 cycle peak: 9.64%

  • 2024 cycle peak: 3.11%

  • Current reading: 1.75%

Drawdowns are compressing as well. Previous bear markets routinely saw 80–90% peak-to-trough declines, whereas the current cycle's deepest retracement from its all-time high above $125,000 to roughly $60,000 represents a drawdown just above 50%.

Each successive halving cycle is delivering lower volatility, shallower drawdowns, and smaller upside. These are signature traits of an asset class maturing and absorbing deeper pools of institutional capital.

What makes this cycle structurally different is that Bitcoin broke to a new ATH before the halving, not after.

In every prior cycle, the halving occurred while Bitcoin was still well below the previous peak. In this cycle, the approval of U.S. spot Bitcoin ETFs in January 2024 pulled the breakout forward, with BTC surpassing its prior high before the halving even took place.

This front-running of the supply event reshuffled the cycle's internal clock. If the top was pulled forward, why not the bottom?

  • Scenario A: Already in. The $60,000 area tested earlier this year may have already marked a significant support zone for this cycle.

  • Scenario B: Still ahead, but sooner. The trough arrives between now and Q4 2026, potentially below $60,000, driven by evolving macro conditions and shifting market narratives.

  • Scenario C: On schedule. The lowlands around Q4 2026 or later, broadly in line with historical four-year templates.

The structural shift demands that market participants prepare for multiple scenarios rather than anchoring to a single cycle narrative.

The Institutions Behind Strategy's Accumulation

Strategy (MSTR) disclosed on April 20 its third-largest single BTC purchase on record: 34,164 BTC for $2.54 billion.

  • Total holdings: 815,061 BTC

  • Cumulative cost: $61.56 billion

  • Avg. cost: $75,527

Year-to-date, Strategy has added roughly 80,000 BTC, surpassing BlackRock's IBIT to become the single largest BTC holder globally.

The engine behind this pace is STRC; it funded over 85% of the latest purchase. With plans to shift STRC distributions from monthly to semi-monthly, Strategy is tightening the feedback loop: more frequent payouts attract more capital, which flows directly into more Bitcoin. It is, in effect, a self-reinforcing accumulation machine.

The more important question is not how much BTC Strategy is buying, but who is funding the vehicle that enables it.

Wall Street's largest institutions are operating through multiple channels simultaneously.

  • Institutional investors collectively own roughly 53–60% of MSTR.

  • Strategy's Executive Chairman Michael J. Saylor personally holds 19.99M shares of Class B stock (9.9% ownership, 45% voting power), further aligning insider incentives with the accumulation strategy.

  • On the STRC side, the holder base is similarly institutional: iShares Trust (BlackRock), American Funds, Vanguard ETFs, and Fidelity ETFs are among the top holders.

This creates a multi-layered institutional exposure structure. These firms are not merely issuing their own spot Bitcoin ETFs; they are simultaneously holding Strategy equity and purchasing STRC, effectively channeling capital into Bitcoin through multiple vectors at once.

Institutions are not just holding. They are actively adding, even with Bitcoin in the $60K–$75K range. In just the past week:

  • Capital Group: +$747M in MSTR (40% increase), total position now above $1.78B

  • Vanguard (VOE): +1.21M MSTR shares ($195M), total position now $255M

Short-term price action can be misleading. But the capital structure tells a different story: the world's largest asset managers and investment banks are not reducing exposure. They are building it through increasingly sophisticated, multi-instrument channels.

Institutional and BTC LTH Are Building Positions

ETF Flows: Strong and sustained institutional inflows.

Spot BTC ETFs have decisively flipped back to net inflows since early April. As of April 21, BTC ETFs have recorded 6 consecutive days of net inflows, with April 17 posting the strongest single-day inflow of 2026 at +$663.9M.

Spot ETH ETFs (ETH-USD) show a similar trend on a smaller scale. 9 consecutive days of net inflows out of the past 12 trading days, the longest streak of 2026. Total net assets have recovered from $10B during the February drawdown to $14B.

On-chain: Long-term holders are re-accumulating.

Since early April, Bitcoin's Long-Term Holder (LTH, >155 days) total supply has begun trending upward again. In the past 7 days, LTH supply has shown a notable increase, now sitting at approximately 16M BTC.

Historically, this pattern signals that experienced holders view current prices as attractive, and it has preceded significant rallies in prior cycles. For reference, the two most recent LTH supply peaks occurred in late September 2024 and late June 2025. In both cases, BTC saw meaningful upside afterward.

Shorts Under Pressure, Second Squeeze Loading

This week's derivatives picture has been dominated by short liquidations.

The largest came on April 17, when Iran ceasefire progress and the reopening of the Strait of Hormuz triggered a rapid move from $73K to $78K, resulting in the week's heaviest short squeeze.

Across the week, short liquidations have consistently outpaced longs, confirming that the prevailing leveraged positioning was against the rally.

The conditions for a second squeeze are building. Two indicators suggest the market remains structurally loaded on the short side:

  1. Funding rates remain negative. The OI-weighted funding rate has been persistently negative since mid-April, though it is gradually compressing toward zero. Looking across timeframes: 30-day avg. -0.319%, 7-day avg. -0.074%, 1-day -0.012%. Shorts are slowly bleeding, paying to maintain positions that are increasingly under water.

  2. The long/short ratio (by account) is still below 1. And the Top Trader long/short ratio at 0.55, indicating institutions remain net short.

The $79–80K zone is a strong resistance level where institutional shorts are heavily concentrated. The first attempt this week failed to break through, pulling back to $78.3K in a typical post-squeeze repricing phase.

However, BTC is currently highly correlated with U.S. equities and sensitive to macro headlines, meaning a breakout is entirely possible. Whether price can hold above $80K, however, will largely depend on how the macro picture evolves.

Week Ahead

  • Apr 28-29: FOMC meeting and press conference

  • Apr 28: Robinhood Q1 2026 earnings release after market close

  • Apr 29: Meta, Microsoft, and Alphabet earnings releases after market close

  • Apr 30: U.S. Q1 2026 GDP advance estimate and March PCE inflation release

Next week’s tone will be shaped by the interaction between macro policy signals and large cap earnings risk.

The Fed remains the primary volatility event, but the market will also need to digest a dense earnings cluster from Robinhood, Meta, Microsoft, and Alphabet across April 28 and April 29. That combination matters for crypto because it directly affects broad risk sentiment, AI and cloud leadership expectations, and the strength of retail trading activity.

The April 30 GDP and PCE releases would then become the confirmation layer, determining whether the post-event move evolves into continuation or reverses into a broader risk reset.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

data.title: EUR/USD Drifted Down To 1.1665/1635 Key Support For Potential Bullish Reversal
data.description:
By Kelvin Wong

The EUR/USD (EUR:USD) hit a recent two-month high of 1.1849 printed on last Friday, April 17, 2026, and turned soft due to the stalled second round of US-Iran peace talks, where it declined by 1.5% to hit a low of 1.1669 on Thursday, April 23, 2026.

Secondly, cracks have appeared in the extended ceasefire agreement, where both sides are using force to prevent oil tankers from transiting the Strait of Hormuz, which led to a 10% plus rally in oil prices, increasing the appeal of short-term safe haven demand status on the US dollar.

Interestingly, the EUR/USD’s five-day decline from its recent two-month high has reached an inflection area for a potential bullish reversal from a technical analysis and intermarket perspective.

Let’s uncover these factors in greater detail.

Eurozone/US implied interest rate policy curve spread has steepened

The monthly implied future policy interest rate curves for the eurozone and the US are calculated using short-term interest rate futures that are highly sensitive to the expectations from these countries’ central banks' monetary policies

The current eurozone/US implied interest rate policy curve spread for the period from May 2026 to September 2026 has steepened from three months ago, with the current September 2026 reading standing at -1.32% compared to -1.37% three months ago (see Fig. 1).

These observations suggest that the ECB is likely to be less dovish or more hawkish than the Fed, which in turn could provide support for a potentially firmer EUR/USD.

Let’s now focus on the potential short-term trajectory (1-3 days) of EUR/USD.

EUR/USD - Minor uptrend phase from March 13, 2026 low remains intact

Despite the five-day decline seen in the EUR/USD, its price actions are still trading above the 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages.

Watch the 1.1665/1635 key short-term pivotal support, and a clearance above 1.1722 (potential upside trigger) may see the next intermediate resistances coming in at 1.1790, 1.1835, and 1.1890 (also a Fibonacci extension) (see Fig. 2).

On the other hand, failure to hold at 1.1635 and an hourly close below it invalidates the bullish reversal scenario to see a continuation of the corrective decline to expose the next intermediate supports at 1.1575 and 1.1510.

Key elements to support the near-term bullish bias on EUR/USD

  • The five-day decline of the EUR/USD has reached the intersection area of the 20-day, 50-day, and 200-day moving averages, where the 20-day MA is now shaping an impending bullish crossover above the 50-day MA.
  • The price actions of the EUR/USD have continued to oscillate within its minor ascending channel in place since the March 30, 2026 low.
  • The hourly RSI momentum indicator has just shaped a bullish divergence condition at its oversold region on Thursday, April 23, 2026.
